name: Ginger
summary: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) is a rhizome monograph focused on the unspecified used in herbal preparations. It is associated with Ginger does not occur in the wild; it is believed to have originated in Southeast Asia and is now cultivated across the humid tropics with India as the largest producer【418916103730052†L148-L163】. Ginger rhizomes contain phenolic constituents such as gingerols, shogaols, zingerone and paradols. Ginger is widely consumed as a food and supplement and is generally regarded as safe.
description: Ginger is represented here as a monograph-style entry centered on the unspecified. Key reported compounds include [6]-gingerol; shogaols; zingerone; paradols. Typical preparation forms: The rhizome is used fresh, dried, pickled, preserved, candied or powdered. Supplements include encapsulated dried powders and extracts【418916103730052†L176-L189】. Ginger rhizomes contain phenolic constituents such as gingerols, shogaols, zingerone and paradols. These compounds modulate immune‑cell function and regulate signalling pathways including nuclear factor‑κB, MAPK and PI3K/Akt/mTOR, and they can activate AMP‑activated protein kinase (AMPK), NRF2, HO‑1 and PPARγ【69571933386645†L153-L169】【69571933386645†L240-L249】. Through these mechanisms ginger exhibits anti‑inflammatory, antioxidant and neuroprotective effects. Interaction profile: Ginger may increase the risk of bleeding when taken with anticoagulant or antiplatelet drugs and could lower blood glucose or blood pressure, potentiating antidiabetic or antihypertensive medications. Use caution or avoid in: Use cautiously in pregnancy, gallstone disease or bleeding disorders. Discontinue before surgery and avoid high‑dose supplements when taking anticoagulants.

These compounds modulate immune‑cell function and regulate signalling pathways including nuclear factor‑κB, MAPK and PI3K/Akt/mTOR, and they can activate AMP‑activated protein kinase (AMPK), NRF2, HO‑1 and PPARγ【69571933386645†L153-L169】【69571933386645†L240-L249】. Through these mechanisms ginger exhibits anti‑inflammatory, antioxidant and neuroprotective effects.
safetyNotes: Ginger is widely consumed as a food and supplement and is generally regarded as safe. Research suggests it may help with mild nausea and menstrual cramps. Reported side effects at high doses include abdominal discomfort, heartburn, diarrhoea and irritation of the mouth or throat【799387966105800†L29-L56】. Clinical studies using up to 1 000 mg/day of dried powdered extract report adverse event rates similar to placebo【739669106228210†L96-L133】. The safety of high‑dose ginger during pregnancy has not been conclusively established.
